description:
You are an automation-workflow specialist who has just joined an on-going project.  
A colleague has left you a single text file that lists sample images to be processed, but the rest of the expected workflow directory tree is missing.  
Your job is to put the proper structure in place, rename the sample file, create a stub shell script, and write a short log so the team’s CI system can confirm everything was set up correctly.

Required final state of the system
1. A top-level directory called  
   /home/user/workflows  
   must exist.

2. Inside /home/user/workflows create a sub-directory named image_cleanup that contains exactly three sub-directories:  
   • input  
   • output  
   • temp

3. The file that already exists at  
   /home/user/sample_images.txt  
   must be moved into /home/user/workflows/image_cleanup/input/  
   and renamed to  
   files_to_process.txt

4. Inside /home/user/workflows/image_cleanup/ create a shell script named run.sh that  
   • is executable by the current user  
   • contains one single line (no extra blank lines) with the following exact text:  
     #!/usr/bin/env bash

5. Produce a plain-text log file at  
   /home/user/workflows/setup.log  
   whose contents must match *exactly* the format shown below (except for the timestamp, which must be the actual current time when you run the commands):

```
SETUP COMPLETE
Timestamp: YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS
Directories:
- /home/user/workflows/image_cleanup
- /home/user/workflows/image_cleanup/input
- /home/user/workflows/image_cleanup/output
- /home/user/workflows/image_cleanup/temp
Files:
- /home/user/workflows/image_cleanup/run.sh (executable)
- /home/user/workflows/image_cleanup/input/files_to_process.txt
```

Formatting rules for setup.log:
• “SETUP COMPLETE” must be the very first line, written in all caps.  
• The second line must begin with “Timestamp: ” followed by the current date and time in the exact 24-hour format YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS (for example, 2025-04-09 14:07:55).  
• Every subsequent line must appear exactly as shown, including leading “- ” bullets and paths.  
• Do not include any additional lines or trailing spaces.

Success criteria the automated test will check
• Every directory and file exists exactly where specified.  
• /home/user/workflows/image_cleanup/run.sh has executable permission for the current user and contains only the correct shebang line.  
• /home/user/workflows/image_cleanup/input/files_to_process.txt exists and is the renamed original file.  
• /home/user/workflows/setup.log matches the required format and lists precisely the same paths (no extra or missing items).  
• The timestamp line is syntactically correct (YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS) but does not need to match a fixed value.

Perform whatever terminal commands you need to reach this final state.
truth:
Initial environment:
1. A file exists at /home/user/sample_images.txt with the following exact content:
```
raw_image_01.jpg
raw_image_02.jpg
```
(No trailing newline after the second line.)

Expected environment after successful task completion:
1. Directory tree:
   /home/user/workflows/
   └── image_cleanup/
       ├── input/
       │   └── files_to_process.txt  (content identical to original sample_images.txt)
       ├── output/
       └── temp/

2. File: /home/user/workflows/image_cleanup/run.sh
   • Permissions: at least ‑rw-xr--- (u+x must be set; group/other don’t matter)
   • Content (single line, no newline at end recommended but acceptable):
     #!/usr/bin/env bash

3. Log file: /home/user/workflows/setup.log
   • First line: “SETUP COMPLETE”
   • Second line: starts with “Timestamp: ” followed by a valid 19-character timestamp matching regex: ^Timestamp: \d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2} \d{2}:\d{2}:\d{2}$
   • Lines 3-7 exactly as shown in the public task description (including spaces and hyphens).

Validation script will:
• Check existence and names of all directories/files.
• Verify run.sh has correct shebang and executable bit.
• Compare contents of files_to_process.txt with original sample_images.txt.
• Validate setup.log content line-for-line after normalising the variable timestamp against the regex.
name: task_000000_3d6b51f6
